title: Penn State track and field earns several qualifying marks at NCAA East First Round
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
Everything was on the line as Penn State traveled to Lexington, Kentucky, for the NCAA East First Round, awaiting the results of 32 athletes with a chance to qualify for the NCAA Championships.

The Nittany Lions kicked off Day 1 of the qualifying meet on Wednesday with the men’s preliminary races and a handful of field events, booking three tickets to the NCAA Championship and seven spots in Friday’s finals.

Wednesday

Graduate student Collin Burkhart opened the action in the men’s shot put on Wednesday, looking to earn his first trip to the NCAA Championship in his final collegiate season.

After throws of 66.32 meters and 67.14 meters in his first two attempts, Burkhart threw for 67.42 meters with his final chance, earning the sixth-best mark and his first-ever ticket to the big stage.

In his first collegiate season, freshman Nolan Carey qualified for the regional meet with the second-best javelin throw in school history. However, he finished one place shy of a championship berth, throwing 66.08 meters for 13th place.

Penn State earned another pair of NCAA Championship tickets in the men’s shot put, where graduate student Dylan Lambrecht set the No. 5 mark in school history with a throw of 19.58 meters. Following his second place throw, senior Ryan Henry also qualified for the big stage, throwing 19.02 meters for fifth, while freshman Bryce Thompson finished in 21st with 18.26 meters.

After missing the NCAA Championship by one place during the indoor season, junior Nick Sloff found himself one step closer to qualifying in the men’s 1,500-meters, running 3:43.11 to finish second in the prelims and earn a spot in Friday’s finals.

Also competing on Friday is sophomore Ajani Dwyer, who set a school record during the 100-meter prelims. Dwyer ran 9.98 seconds for another second place finish, setting a new record for the third time this season.

In the men’s 400-meters, Jake Palermo had a bounce-back performance after failing to qualify for the Big Ten Championship finals earlier this month.

Palermo ran 45.25 seconds at the NCAA East First Round to finish fifth and book Penn State’s third ticket to Friday’s final round.

Penn State’s “800m U” legacy continued in the regional meet on Wednesday, as four athletes qualified for the men’s 800-meter finals.

Senior Niko Schultz led the way with a time of 1:46.17 for second place, while reigning Big Ten champion Allon Clay finished in third in 1:46.55. Yukichi Ishii qualified after missing out on the Big Ten finals, running 1:46.91 for ninth place, while junior Andrew Regnier earned the last spot for the Nittany Lions in 1:47.03 for 11th place.

Kaleb Nastari missed a spot in the finals, finishing in 31st place in 1:48.55.

Up next

Eleven women’s athletes will compete on Thursday for a spot in Saturday’s finals and the NCAA Championship, kicking off with Alex Pancoast in the hammer throw at 10 a.m.

title: Penn State track and field's Ajani Dwyer sets 100m school record
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
For the third time this season, Ajani Dwyer is rewriting the history books.

Dwyer set the Penn State men's 100-meter record in the NCAA East First Round prelims, running a personal-best 9.98 seconds

The Sewell, New Jersey, native earned a spot in the 100m finals on Friday, where the top 12 finishers will earn a ticket to the NCAA Championship.

Two weeks ago, Dwyer ran 9.93 seconds in the 100m finals at the Big Ten Championship, but wasn't counted as a record due to wind interference.

title: Former Penn State WR Irvin Charles traded to Seattle Seahawks
author: Andrew Deal | The Daily Collegian
description:
A former Nittany Lion is heading to the reigning Super Bowl champions.

Wide receiver Irvin Charles has been traded from the New York Jets to the Seattle Seahawks, according to Adam Schefter. The Jets received a conditional 2028 7th-round pick in return.

Trade: Seahawks traded for former Jets wide receiver and special teams standout Irvin Charles in exchange for a 2028 conditional seventh-round pick.— Adam Schefter (@AdamSchefter) May 27, 2026

Charles played for the Nittany Lions from 2016-17, totaling three catches for 110 yards and a touchdown. He returned to football in 2021, suiting up for Division II school IUP, where he caught 39 passes for 792 yards and 12 touchdowns.

The former Penn Stater carved out a role on the Jets as a standout special teams player, playing in 25 games in 2023 and 2024.

Charles will join the Seahawks with a chance to make the team and earn a role on one of the strongest special teams units in the league.

title: Kickoff times, networks announced for Penn State's first 3 games
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The kickoff times and networks for Penn State’s first three games of the 2026 season have been released.

Week 1's matchup against Marshall will kickoff at 3:30 p.m. ET on Sept. 5 with FS1 as the broadcaster. It will be the second-consecutive year that Penn State will begin its season with a 3:30 p.m. start time.

Penn State will face Temple the following week at noon on ESPN2. The two sides will face off at Lincoln Financial Field, marking their first contest since 2015.

The Nittany Lions game against Buffalo in Week 3 will begin at noon on Big Ten Network.

Penn State's matchup against Northwestern on Oct. 2 is the only other game so far that has received a set kickoff time, as the two are set face off at 8 p.m. ET.

While an official game time hasn't been announced for Penn State's Big Ten opener against Wisconsin, the Nittany Lions do know the contest won't kickoff later than 5 p.m. ET.

title: Film review | 3-star TE Sean Currie brings height advantage, athleticism to Penn State’s 2027 class
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State earned its second tight end commitment in the Class of 2027 when 3-star Sean Currie announced his commitment on Friday afternoon via the Rivals YouTube channel.

Currie ranks as the No. 15 player out of New Jersey and No. 32 tight end in the 2027 class per 247Sports composite. He had a strong junior season at The Lawrenceville School, closing out the year with 34 receptions for 474 yards and eight touchdowns. He also played some edge, recording three sacks and two tackles for loss.

Additionally, Currie’s background as a former highly regarded lacrosse recruit lends to him being an elite athlete, as he possesses not only the sheer size of being 6-foot-7 and 230 pounds, but also the mobility that’s required at the tight end position.

His film showcases his ability to use his size to his advantage by snagging passes that shorter targets might not, while making his presence felt on the line as a blocker.

A towering frame that remains mobile

Currie towers over many of his adversaries, and he uses that height for his benefit. In one-on-one matchups, his wingspan makes it easier to get separation. He also has a longer reach toward the ball, allowing him to make catches over his shoulders and out in front of him more comfortably against smaller competition.

As it can be more difficult to maneuver in the same ways that a smaller player can, size of that degree can become a weakness. But Currie has rare speed and agility for his size that beats defenders off the snap, and he works himself into open space up the middle and along the sidelines.

Currie is an elite route runner that attacks open space with a swiftness unbecoming of his size, and once he's got the ball with nothing but green grass in front of him, he's tough to track down. pic.twitter.com/xtVtKupGCY— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Covering him is also difficult on passing plays because of Currie’s jumping capabilities. He has strong legs and swiftly reaches his peak, and his soft hands make him a reliable receiver against noticeably shorter cornerbacks and safeties.

Once he’s got the ball with room to run, his long strides can carry him into the end zone. But even with remaining opposition, Currie has no problem bulldozing through for extra yardage.

When attempting to tackle him, any solo attempt above the waist is a no-go, forcing the knees and ankles to be the only method of taking him down.

Currie is an elite route runner that attacks open space with a swiftness unbecoming of his size, and once he's got the ball with nothing but green grass in front of him, he's tough to track down. pic.twitter.com/xtVtKupGCY— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Safe hands from all areas

Currie is a fundamentally sound pass catcher that won’t drop the early ones, something that can help him thrive in the college game.

Passes often come around his hips rather than at his chest, being a larger target than the majority of options. However, Currie consistently secures the ball with safe hands and isn’t afraid to bend over to make a catch before slamming into contact.

As a taller target, sometimes passes come a little low. But he's comfortable catching from any range. Currie also possesses grit and isn't afraid to hit the turf if it means getting into the end zone. pic.twitter.com/Aetldvlzcr— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Currie’s long wingspan also allows him more space for the quarterback to throw a leading pass out in front of him. When that pass is slightly too far ahead, Currie gets creative with how he adapts — and he has previously made flashy one-handed catches.

Currie is also a creative pass catcher and will attempt one-handed grabs that have ended in flashy touchdowns in the past, featuring his ability to adapt on the fly and make the most out of any pass.Here's a pair of touchdowns showcasing his one-handed ability: pic.twitter.com/2jW9ohSjO9— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

He’s at his best when the pass is higher, as he can easily get up for a grab. Currie will bring down previously uncatchable passes because of his natural gift of size and comfortability with his tall frame.

Currie is also proficient in catching over his shoulder and is a quality route runner that works himself into open spaces for uncontested receptions. He’s especially a threat in the back corner of the end zone on corner routes after gathering speed while in motion before the snap.

On one-on-one matchups, Currie's strength and wingspan allow him to bully opponents for separation, making open catches in front of him as well as over his shoulder when he beats the defensive back longwise. pic.twitter.com/6LS3MzjAo4— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

A developing blocker

Currie improved his blocking capabilities greatly between his sophomore and junior seasons, according to 247Sports’ Director of Scouting Andrew Ivins. That enhanced technique pairs nicely with his size, making him a threat when he helps open a path for his running backs.

Currie is a powerful blocker that pushes his opponent back more often than not. He’s strong on initial contact and uses his size to carry momentum forward for leverage when he gets underneath his matchup’s arms.

In the blocking game, Currie is quick to the point of contact and plays his angles well, often using leverage from attacking opposing flanks to bulldoze a path for his backs. pic.twitter.com/YAhyaYWGLA— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

He’s also smart with his angles and possesses great vision when deciding who to block. Currie will step back, maneuver to the strong side and plan an extra step ahead in his progression to see who on the defense is able to make a move on the ball carrier.

In addition, Currie goes through progressions well when figuring out who to block. When he recognizes that the first layer is secure, he fills the gap to block the secondary and gives his backs extra yardage. pic.twitter.com/kkHpXpN8KU— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

Currie’s ability to recognize where he’s needed at those particular moments creates additional space for running backs and can be the difference between a short gain and a first down.

When he’s not the target downfield, Currie will continue to block after the play is made and keep defenders at bay while his teammates reap the extra yards. There have been times when Currie’s lead blocking has resulted in a touchdown on plays where it wasn’t necessarily guaranteed.

Currie, when not the target on a passing play, continues to make an impact in the blocking game downfield by providing key blocks on extended plays en route to the end zone.Here's Currie fending off a group of trailing defenders while his teammate scores: pic.twitter.com/2yoBoHSEJM— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 26, 2026

title: Conference breakdown | Ranking Big Ten football programs heading into summer
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Big Ten has produced the national champion the last three years, proving the success of the conference year after year.

Surely, 2026 should be no different, as several teams sit atop the conference and are regarded highly by the nation heading into the summer. Here’s a look at where they currently stack up.

Tier 1: Ohio State, Indiana, Oregon

While Ohio State missed out on winning back-to-back national titles when it fell to Miami in the College Football Playoff quarterfinals last year, the Buckeyes aren’t expected to fall far from last year’s position.

Redshirt sophomore quarterback Julian Sayin will take the reins for his second year as a starter. He will still be throwing to star wide receiver Jeremiah Smith, who is a strong Heisman Trophy candidate and will likely be suiting up for his final season with the Buckeyes.

One of Ohio State’s biggest challengers is reigning national champion Indiana. While the Hoosiers are without Heisman-winning quarterback Fernando Mendoza, they have many other pieces of its squad returning, including wide receiver Charlie Becker and left tackle Carter Smith, who won Big Ten Offensive Lineman of the Year.

Indiana also attacked the transfer portal, bringing in former Nittany Lion cornerback A.J. Harris and TCU quarterback Josh Hoover to take over Mendoza’s position.

Oregon also had a big run in the playoffs last season, falling to the Hoosiers in the Peach Bowl semifinal. Considering quarterback Dante Moore is back for his final season and is joined by speedy wide receiver Evan Stewart, the Ducks could make some noise in the Big Ten this year.

Tier 2: Michigan, Penn State, USC, Washington

A new era of football has emerged at Michigan, with former Utah head coach Kyle Whittingham replacing Sherrone Moore. Sophomore quarterback and former No. 1 overall recruit Bryce Underwood is set to start for his second season, hopefully growing his game under a new coaching staff after a freshman campaign that left much to be desired.

Likewise, Penn State is entering its first season under a new coach for the first time since 2014. However, Matt Campbell has built a complete roster with dozens of transfers from Iowa State, including quarterback Rocco Becht, running back Carson Hansen and wide receivers Chase Sowell and Brett Eskildsen.

The Nittany Lions were just one game away from playing for the national championship two seasons ago, and they still have a few players from that squad, like Tony Rojas, Zion Tracy and Audavion Collins. But with plenty of change since, Penn State is simply looking to make the most out of Year 1 under Campbell.

USC was one of six Big Ten programs to finish in the AP poll, and is expected to stay similar in strength to last year’s 9-4 record. The Trojans return a majority of its roster, including its entire offensive line, and have former TCU head coach Gary Patterson — TCU’s all-time winningest coach — as defensive coordinator.

While Washington made the national championship in 2023, the departure of Kalen DeBoer and its key players from that run has resulted in somewhat of a rebuilding phase with six and nine wins the last two years, respectively.

But with Demond Williams Jr. entering his second year as the starting quarterback and talent out wide with Dezmen Roebuck, the Huskies could have a strong upcoming season in the Big Ten if Williams puts it all together.

Tier 3: UCLA, Iowa, Illinois

UCLA is a rising star in the Big Ten after upsetting Penn State last season and winning the next two games. While it only finished with those three wins on the season, things are expected to be better under new coach Bob Chesney with dual-threat quarterback Nico Iamaleava returning.

Iowa lost starting quarterback Mark Gronowski, who ran out of eligibility, but has two former transfers set to battle it out in Jeremy Hecklinski and Hank Brown. It's unclear who will win the job, but on the other side of the ball, the Hawkeyes' defense is projected to be their strong suit as always.

While Illinois has to adjust to life without starting quarterback Luke Altmyer, there is plenty of young talent on the roster. However, it’s up to them to put the pieces together to continue building off two straight successful campaigns.

Tier 4: Nebraska, Northwestern, Minnesota

Nebraska underwent a significant change during the offseason when two-year starting quarterback Dylan Raiola entered the transfer portal and landed at Oregon. Now, UNLV transfer Anthony Colandrea will likely lead the way for the Cornhuskers in a year that could make or officially break Matt Rhule’s tenure in Lincoln.

While Northwestern is unveiling its state-of-the-art Ryan Field in October, the new stadium might not see too many wins from its home team. Under new offensive coordinator Chip Kelly, the Wildcats’ offense is expected to take a step forward, but likely won’t see too much growth as an overall team.

Minnesota is one of the few teams in the Big Ten who didn’t experience much roster turnover or changes this season and will likely stay similar to last year’s 8-5 record.

Tier 5: Rutgers, Wisconsin, Maryland, Michigan State, Purdue

Rutgers, Wisconsin, Maryland, Michigan State and Purdue each recorded two or less conference wins last season, but there’s hope on the horizon for Wisconsin and Michigan State.

The Spartans have a new coach in Pat Fitzgerald, and the Badgers invested more resources into their program this offseason, so both could see improvements from last year. It’s unlikely, however, that they’ll be competing among the best of the Big Ten.

title: Penn State track and field books 32 entries to NCAA East First Round
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After less-than-favorable outcomes at the Big Ten championships last week, Penn State is off to the next part of its postseason.

The Nittany Lions will compete with the top-48 athletes in each event from Wednesday to Saturday for the top 12 spots and a ticket to the NCAA Championship next month.

Here’s a look at the Nittany Lions who will be competing this week for a spot in the NCAA Championships.

Sprints

For the first time in several years, Penn State has a woman’s entry in the 100-meter and 200-meter at the NCAA East First Round. Marie-Eloise Leclair is seeded in the top-20 in both races, with the first round set for 6 p.m. and 8:45 p.m. on Thursday.

Leclair would have to get through two rounds of action for a spot in the top 24, with the finals slated for 6:35 p.m. and 7:50 p.m. on Saturday. She’s seeded 16th in the 100m and 13th in the 200m — both seeds near a championship appearance — so she’ll have to stay true to her personal bests of 11.12 seconds and 22.75 seconds for her ticket.

On the men’s side, sophomore Ajani Dwyer is set for his first NCAA East First Round in the 100m, with the prelims at 7 p.m. on Wednesday and finals at 6:35 p.m. on Friday.

He’s seeded with a time of 9.96 seconds and the fourth-best mark in the region, but ran an even better time of 9.93 seconds at the Big Ten Championship last weekend.

In the men’s 400-meters, sophomore Jake Palermo is seeded 13th in the region ahead of his prelims at 7:45 p.m. on Wednesday. However, he’s coming off one of the worst performances of his career, when he ran 46.75 seconds and was eliminated in the first round of the conference championship.

Palermo is also part of the men’s 4x400m relay at 8:45 p.m. on Friday, running alongside Carter Fitzgerald, Matt DeMatteo and Peyton Sommers. They’re ranked No. 23 in the region with a time of 3:05.39, and will look for a top-12 finish for a spot on the biggest stage.

Mid-distance/distance

Penn State’s mid-distance success has been on display all season, and that will be showcased with nine athletes in the men’s and women’s 800-meters.

The men’s 800m will kick off at 7:50 p.m. Wednesday and conclude with the finals at 7:05 p.m. on Friday. Big Ten champion Allon Clay leads the way with the No. 1 seed, followed by Niko Schultz in third, Andrew Regnier in eighth, Yukichi Ishii in 10th and Kaleb Nastari in 37th.

On the women’s side, they’ll race at 7:50 p.m. on Thursday and 7:05 p.m. on Saturday. Big Ten champion Hayley Kitching sits 12th in the region, followed by Allison Johnson in 19th, Boh Ritchie in 21st and Tayissa Buchanan in 29th.

Junior Nick Sloff will kick off the men’s 1,500-meters at 6:30 p.m. on Wednesday with the finals at 5:15 p.m. on Friday. He comes into the race with the No. 18 mark at 3:39.56.

In the women’s 1,500m, senior Lea Hatcher is ranked No. 46 in the region and will compete at 6:30 p.m. on Thursday and 5:15 p.m. on Saturday.

Senior Claire Daniels has Penn State’s lone 10,000-meter entry, racing at 9:10 p.m. on Thursday. She has the 19th-best mark with a season-best 33:18.60.

Ryan Watson earned a spot in the men’s 3,000-meter steeplechase at 5:40 p.m. on Friday with the No. 22 seed, while Allison Dougherty has a spot in the women’s steeplechase at 5:40 p.m. on Saturday with the No. 41 seed.

Jumps

Penn State has a trio of athletes qualified to compete in the high jump and another athlete in the pole vault this week.

Freshman Trip Campbell is seeded with the No. 32 mark in the men’s high jump, competing at 2 p.m. on Saturday. His mark is just ahead of teammate Robert Allen’s mark of 2.12 meters, which is the No. 42 seed.

In the women’s high jump, sophomore Hannah Riolo is seeded 13th with a season-best mark of 1.80 meters — the seventh-best in school history — and will compete at 2 p.m. on Saturday.

Senior Tristan McGarrah just made the qualifying mark in the men’s pole vault, jumping 5.20 meters for the 46th-best mark in the region. He’ll likely need a big personal best to qualify for the NCAA Championships, when he competes at 6:30 p.m. on Wednesday.

Throws

In Darrell Hill’s first season at the helm with Penn State throws, he has nine athletes qualified for the NCAA East First Round.

Graduate student Collin Burkhart will kick off the throwing portion of the competition in the men’s hammer throw at 10 a.m. on Wednesday. He has the 12th-best mark in the region with 67.53 meters, good for No. 3 in school history.

Freshman Nolan Carey will have his shot at qualifying for the NCAA Championships in the men’s javelin at 1 p.m. on Friday, boasting the No. 6 mark ahead of the competition.

In the men’s shot put, the Nittany Lions have three athletes in the top-20 and in position to qualify for the NCAA Championships when they compete at 6:30 p.m. on Wednesday. Senior Ryan Henry leads the way with the No. 6 mark in 19.48 meters, followed by Dylan Lambrecht in seventh and freshman Bryce Thompson with the No. 16 mark.

Senior Alex Pancoast will kick off the women’s throws at 10 a.m. on Thursday, where she has the No. 36 mark in the conference. She’ll be followed by Audrey Friedman at 1 p.m. in the javelin throw, where she has the 31st-best mark.

The final throws event comes in the women’s shot put, where freshman Skye Petrie-Cameron will compete as the No. 17 seed and sophomore Sasha Garnett as the No. 23 seed. The two will throw at 6:30 p.m. on Thursday.

title: Penn State baseball sophomore pitcher Harrison Lollin enters transfer portal
author: Myles Hannak | The Daily Collegian
description:
After a rough year on the mound, the blue and white took another beating.

The Nittany Lions posted the Big Ten's worst ERA last season, and last year's blue and white leader in the stat, Harrison Lollin, announced his decision to enter the transfer portal.

The Monroe Township, New Jersey, native was a former top-ten recruit from his state, but struggled during his freshman year with an ERA over 10. This season, however, Lollin experienced a return to form, with 11 total appearances and a 4.05 ERA.

This end-of-year figure marked the only blue and white arm in the regular bullpen to record an ERA under five. Lollin uses an arsenal of five pitches, a low 90s fastball, a cutter, slider, curveball and changeup, which gave him a much larger selection than last year's bullpen options.

Lollin now looks for a change of scenery for the remainder of his collegiate career and to build off that already strong opportunity.

title: COLUMN | Penn State Athletics’s worst moments in historically bad year
author: Myles Hannak | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State athletics finished 233-188-9 in the 2025-26 school year, marking its worst finish since 2018-19 at 231-183-14.

In the five most popular sports — softball, men’s and women’s basketball, football and baseball — the Nittany Lions posted a .293 win percentage, a bottom-five figure out of all Power 4 schools.

Here are the worst moments from a difficult year for the program.

​Men’s basketball season, Senior Night

We’ll start with where I spent most of last year: the BJC media section, covering one of the longest, most grueling seasons. Penn State men's basketball suffered its worst defensive season of all time, and it seemed unlikely to win a conference game until it pulled off a few miracles.

This team wasn’t expected to be good, but a few moments especially stood out to affirm that: Pitt's utterly embarrassing blowout win in Hershey; Lamar Wilkinson's Hoosier three-point record against Penn State; Diggy Coit torching the Nittany Lions; and various role players having career games at Happy Valley.

There was a real low of the lows when it came to the hardwood: Senior Night against rival Ohio State. Team leader Josh Reed was honored, but put up one of his worst games all season, and the Nittany Lions were embarrassed in front of their own crowd.

The men's basketball season best illustrates the department's challenges — though the administration seems to notice this is a priority, as significant investment is on the way.

Women’s basketball’s season of failure

Staying on the hardwood, we’ll go to the women's team, which wasted the talents of two top players, Kiyomi McMiller and Gracie Merkle.

The Nittany Lions failed to secure a Big Ten tournament bid and posted one of their worst seasons. Subsequently, long-tenured coach Carolyn Keiger was fired, and alum Tanisha Wright took the helm of the program in March.

Women’s volleyball’s national championship defense

Men's and women's basketball had modest expectations and largely met them, but the women's volleyball team's title defense failed to meet any of them. It posted the sharpest win-loss drop of all Penn State teams, down 35.2% from last season.

Significant roster turnover meant the blue and white couldn't replicate last year's performance. The team faced tough stretches, including two three-match losing streaks and various upsets that sapped all momentum.​

Baseball’s fan collapse

For my fourth addition to the list, the baseball team really seemed to hit a wall in the third year under coach Mike Gambino.

After making the Big Ten Tournament championship game in his first season, then improving the team's win total to 33 and reaching the tournament semifinals last year, it seemed the expectation was that this program was on the up and up. Yet they had one of their worst finishes in recent history this season and were not competitive in most of their Big Ten games.

The baseball team is one of the programs still developing at Penn State, and Gambino should have some leeway. It wasn’t the end of the world, but from a fan perspective, this season came pretty close.

As I said, the results didn't go the right way in the first year of the new student section, “Section 814.” That inaugural bunch had to endure a year full of disappointment, with its coveted dollar dog promotions routinely canceled by grueling weather.

Throw in the ongoing situation between the State College Spikes and the university regarding Medlar Field at Lubrano Park, and everything with the baseball program this year just went… well, not well.

Gavin McKenna’s tenure

For the worst Penn State athletic moment this year — and this may come as a shock — I have the Gavin McKenna tenure.

The Whitehorse, Yukon, native was the most anticipated recruit in Division I hockey’s recent memory, and he arrived with sky-high expectations. Many fans believed his talent could help the team reach new heights and deliver a standout season after the team’s 2025 Frozen Four appearance.

And in some ways, McKenna did live up to that hype. On an individual-level, he broke the Penn State freshman record with 51 points and posted a historic eight-point outing against Ohio State.

However, even with McKenna’s offensive production, the Nittany Lions finished the regular season with a 21-14-2 record, a finish that, despite the numbers, included losses in which Penn State was unable to get over the hump against other more established programs.

Most notably from the Mitten State, where Michigan and Michigan State went a combined 8-1 over the blue and white. Even McKenna’s Happy Valley debut, which ended in a 6-4 loss to Clarkson, and his swan song, in which Penn State fell apart to end the year, went badly.

Throughout the last two months of play, Penn State went 4-7, a record that extended into the postseason, where the program again went home trophyless.

Not only did the season fall short of expectations, but off-the-ice issues also cast a shadow over McKenna's short tenure. The infamous Beaver Stadium game saw McKenna score an iconic goal, but Penn State still lost 5-4 in overtime to Michigan State, a stinging defeat in front of a record crowd.

Just under a week later, McKenna was arrested on a felony aggravated assault charge from an incident that occurred the night of the defeat.

Ultimately, the combination of unmet team expectations and legal controversy made McKenna's one-year stay feel like a squandered opportunity that didn’t reach anywhere near the levels it should’ve.

title: Film review | 3-star DL Aniti Paiva holds size, power
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
Penn State earned a commitment from 3-star defensive lineman Aniti Paiva on Friday, making him the first Nittany Lion pledge from Utah this century.

Paiva is a big-bodied defensive lineman, playing in the interior as a 6-foot-2, 315-pound tackle. He ranks as the No. 1017 player in the country, No. 112 defensive lineman and No. 12 player in Utah.

He shores up the interior of the defensive line as Penn State continues to transition to bigger defensive linemen on the inside.

Here’s what sticks out on Paiva’s tape.

Collegiate size

Paiva is well over 300 pounds, which is not often the case in young Nittany Lion defensive tackles and its starters.

Last season, Penn State’s three main defensive tackles — Zane Durant, Alonzo Ford Jr. and Xavier Gilliam — were all at or under 300 pounds. Entering his senior year of high school, Paiva is 315 pounds.

Paiva already has collegiate size, standing at 6-foot-2, 315 pounds When he’s taking on undersized linemen like these two plays, he gets a quick push, overmatching the center pic.twitter.com/OMboCnlKMC— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 24, 2026

Paiva proved when he goes against undersized linemen, he can quickly win those battles and get into the backfield. This often comes into play in the run game, where he utilized his size to his advantage to stuff a rush behind the line of scrimmage.

Block eater

While a lot of his plays aren’t flashy, he could fly under the radar as someone who helps out the rest of his line.

In high school, he’s dealing with significantly smaller offensive linemen most of the time, but his power to eat up blocks to allow his other pass rushers one-on-one opportunities is a silent but strong attribute to have.

Paiva also showed capabilities of eating blocks, giving his teammates 1-on-1 opportunities While it isn’t flashy, it’s a reliable skill to help the overall success of the defense pic.twitter.com/YnkPMJg3jO— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 24, 2026

Whether it’s in the run game or the pass game, Paiva has dealt with a double team, and even a triple team, which instantly gives favorable situations around him. With the likes of Yvan Kemajou and Max Granville — two young, quick pass rushers — the value of a big defensive tackle who can take on blocks is even higher.

His toughness to take on double or even triple teams makes for instant favorable matchups for his defensive ends Because of this, the other pass rushers are able to slow down the runs and the plays that are away from him pic.twitter.com/XhhNwZ4Z4A— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 24, 2026

Even with Paiva taking on multiple blockers, he still finds ways to win. With his size and ability to win at the line, he’ll fit right in with an interior defensive line room that holds similar builds.

Sticks with plays

Alongside his talent, Paiva has shown heart and a mindset that pushes him to continue on until the whistle has blown.

This allows him to track down a running back and to stuff the line of scrimmage no matter the play. It’s a skill that often can’t be taught, as he fought through blocks no matter how long the play was.

Paiva is an ultimate run stuffer no matter the attention he receives His 315-pounds frame in addition to his power allows him to stuff the line and shut down run plays pic.twitter.com/g1WAOX9wAz— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 24, 2026

Paiva’s size allows him to become an ultimate run stuffer, shedding blocks to prevent a positive gain.

His never-give-up mentality in addition to his collegiate size makes him a large threat to offensive lines, getting a lot of attention during run plays.

Paiva’s relentless mentality makes it even more difficult for offensive linemen to contain him This mentality shows that he doesn’t stop until the whistle is blown, still making a noticeable impact pic.twitter.com/coC5H5TTMK— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 24, 2026

Paiva is a prototypical nose tackle, but something Penn State hasn’t truly had in years. His size coupled with his willingness to take on multiple blockers, could give him a chance to play early in his career — especially given that multiple current defensive linemen have just one year of eligibility remaining.

title: 'We won't sit quietly' | Local groups advocate for Addison Court amid redevelopment uncertainty
author: Jonathon Chiu | The Daily Collegian
description:
As talks ramp up to have Addison Court — a low-income senior housing facility in State College — sold and redeveloped, local advocacy groups are advocating for its preservation.

The complex, which opened in 1994 to provide affordable housing to elderly and disabled residents downtown, is at risk of being sold and redeveloped by PrimeCore Properties into a mixed-use structure called Encore.

During a Thursday State College Planning Commission meeting, members of the Centre County chapter of the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) and Central PA United joined tenants in emphasizing the need for them to continue to live in State College and preserve the apartment complex.

At the meeting, PrimeCore CEO Ara Kervandjian said they are pursuing a preservation plan for Addison Court over Encore’s construction in response to feedback from an April 20 borough council meeting. This plan would include restoring the building’s status within the Low Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC) program, which expired on Dec. 31, 2024, to ensure the building remains affordable for tenants.

Should this not be possible, Kervandjian said construction of Encore would commence. PrimeCore also remains intent on seeking approval for the height amendment. Local advocacy groups asked the borough to deny that request if asked.

Centre County DSA Member Sylus Smith said in an interview he and others are skeptical about if PrimeCore will actively pursue the preservation plan, given Kervandjian’s emphasis on the potential challenges of securing LIHTC funding.

“This is the first time we’re hearing about it,” Smith, a fourth-year studying political science, said. “(Kervandjian) repeatedly emphasizes, ‘Hey, this is a competitive tax credit to go for,’ and he argues that, because it’s not guaranteed, we should give them the zoning change so they can be prepared for their plan B of demolishing the building.”

Smith said the borough should launch an independent investigation into the costs of renovating Addison Court — another challenge Kervandjian mentioned — and do everything in their power to hold PrimeCore accountable.

“There’s a comprehensive zoning rewrite going on,” Smith said. “Why should we make this minor amendment for the personal benefit of one person’s property rather than proceeding with our comprehensive zoning rewrite as a community and seeing where that goes? We have a process going on, and it’s unnecessary to give up that leverage when we don’t actually know that [Kervandjian] is going to earnestly pursue plan A.”

Ben Lockwood, one of Centre County DSA’s co-chairs, said he became aware of discussions to sell Addison Court from the Centre Daily Times’ coverage of the borough council’s meeting on Dec. 15, 2025. This was followed by efforts to contact tenants.

“A few weeks ago, we knocked on every door in Addison Court and talked to the residents there — they want to stay,” Lockwood said. “What we’ve noticed is that a lot of the organizations, government officials and property owners are speaking as if they know what’s best for the people who live at Addison Court.”

Lockwood said Centre County DSA is working to organize Addison Court’s tenants while also exploring ways to increase public awareness.

Centre County DSA has an ActionNetwork petition addressed to the State College Borough Council, Centre County Commissioners, Beaver Court Associates — Addison Court’s previous owner — and PrimeCore calling for the preservation of Addison Court. The petition has over 180 signatures.

Lockwood said copies of the petition will be delivered to these organizations in the near future.

“No one is fighting on behalf of the residents themselves, so that’s the role we’re going to fill,” Lockwood said.

For Central PA United Chapter Director Jenna Henry, the issue of Addison Court is a personal one, as her mother was a former resident.

“This is one of the only places in State College they can afford, and it is the only place where they can have easy access to buses, grocery stores and the library,” Henry said.

While she saw the petition as a good start, Henry said it shouldn’t remove from the importance of people actively making their voices heard.

“People need to make it clear to the developers and local decision makers that we won't sit quietly while poor people are removed from their homes for no fault of their own,” Henry said. “People need to write letters to the editor, contact their state representatives, show up at the planning commission meetings and State College Borough Council meetings.”

John Harris, a 28-year tenant of Addison Court, said he heard residents received offers to Lions Gate Apartments by the real estate company Continental Real Estate Management, which owns the complex. Continental has been tasked with relocating Addison Court’s residents out of the building, according to Kervandjian.

However, Lions Gate’s cheapest apartment — priced at $1,075 a month — is almost four times more expensive than the $295 Harris currently pays. The majority of Harris’ rent is covered by the Housing Choice Voucher Program, also called Section 8, which Lions Gate does not accept.

Lions Gate’s location at 424 Waupelani Dr. also causes Addison Court’s tenants who choose to move there to be reliant on CATA buses to get to and from downtown State College, especially if they — like Harris — don’t have a car.

“To get there, I would have to hire a moving van,” Harris said. “That would cost money and it was, altogether, unaware of my own situation entirely.”

author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
With the transfer portal open, four former Nittany Lions have entered the portal.

Johnson saw minimal time during his three years with the blue and white, playing in just five games across two seasons, allowing four goals. He was stuck behind full-time starters Jack Fracyon in 2025 and Preston Hawkins in 2026.

Jaszcz is in the portal after seeing action in just two games during his freshman year in 2025. The West Chester, Pennsylvania, native was ranked as a 4-star recruit by Inside Lacrosse.

title: 40 year anniversary special | Penn State’s stat leaders in the 1986 national championship season
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
In order to capture Penn State’s second national championship, Joe Paterno needed each starting player to contribute at a championship level.

The 1986 Nittany Lions were elite on both sides of the football field, with a third-ranked defense and offense ranking in the top-20. They were a complete roster that leaned on one another when a certain aspect of the game plan went awry, giving Penn State different options when it came down to preparing for each individual game.

Four decades after the win over Miami on Jan. 2, 1987 in the Fiesta Bowl, here are the stat category leaders from the Nittany Lions who won it all and their contributions in the biggest moments.

Passing: John Shaffer

John Shaffer experienced what he said was the “most anxiety-ridden, pressure-packed time” of his life during the training camps leading up to the 1986 season due to a quarterback position battle with backup Matt Knizner.

Shaffer’s junior season the year prior ended with him faltering in the Orange Bowl for the only loss of his career, leading to controversy on whether he’d start as a senior.

Shaffer won the job, completing 55.9% of his passes for 1,510 passing yards and nine touchdowns with four interceptions in his second season as the starting quarterback. He finished his career with a 66-1 record.

Although he led the Nittany Lions to yet another undefeated season leading up to the bowl game, his passing game once again faltered in the big moment, going 5-16 for 53 yards against Miami.

However, he had a big completion to wide receiver Eric Hamilton to convert on third-and-12 before later calling his own number to tie the game, 7-7, at halftime.

But Shaffer’s regular season actions were more than satisfactory in a noticeably weak schedule, as Penn State remained independent. Shaffer and company silenced the doubt for good and beat No. 2 Alabama, 23-3, on the road to improve to 7-0, with the passer going 13-17 for 168 yards in arguably the best game of his career.

Rushing: D.J. Dozier

When Shaffer struggled in the passing game, the running backs picked up the slack. And in a season where three Penn State running backs were drafted within the first three rounds of the 1987 NFL Draft, D.J. Dozier was the star of the show.

Down 10-7 in the fourth quarter of the Fiesta Bowl, Dozier took a handoff from Shaffer on the 6-yard line and made an inside cut through an open lane before effortlessly shedding one last defender for the championship-winning touchdown.

But Dozier was used to scoring big, game-clinching touchdowns on the ground. He had already sealed the deal against the Crimson Tide two months back and was ready for the moment to take home the title.

It capped off a solid season for the senior running back who was named a consensus All-American.

While it wasn’t his best year as a Nittany Lion on paper with that honor going to his 1,002 yards as a freshman, it was his best as a multi-skilled back as he dipped more into receiving in 1986.

Despite a refocusing on being a threat in many situations, Dozier finished his senior campaign with 811 rushing yards on 171 attempts and scored a career-high 10 touchdowns. He was later drafted 14th overall in the 1987 NFL Draft by the Minnesota Vikings.

Receiving: Eric Hamilton

The 1980s were a different time, with modern-day rules not yet put in place to allow the passing game to flourish as it does in the 21st century, such as calls like roughing the passer.

However, Eric Hamilton was the receiver with the most receiving yards for the Nittany Lions in the championship season. He racked up 387 yards and four touchdowns, averaging 20.4 yards per reception in a system that spread attempts evenly between him, Dozier, Ray Roundtree and tight end Brian Silverling.

Hamilton was targeted 19 times over the course of his senior season and made the most of it when his name was called. Hamilton averaged 35.2 yards per game in 1986 as a key contributor when the game plan called for a big gain.

Hamilton had an 82-yard receiving touchdown — thrown by Knizner — in Penn State’s 34-14 victory over Pitt in the final game of the regular season to secure a spot in the Fiesta Bowl. The pass was one of the longest passes made in Penn State’s history, currently at the No. 9 spot.

Tackles: Shane Conlan

Penn State’s reputation as “Linebacker U” dates back to the late 1960s, and Shane Conlan’s tenure as the leading defender didn’t disappoint.

Conlan led the Nittany Lions with 79 tackles on the season, 63 of which were solo efforts.

In the Fiesta Bowl, Conlan had eight tackles and a pair of interceptions, making his presence felt in a game that was largely a defensive standoff. The first of his interceptions came in the third quarter and deep in Penn State territory, as Miami quarterback Vinny Testaverde lost his footing in the pocket and threw a weak pass right to Conlan.

It’s the second interception that would go down in history as the play that won Penn State the national championship. While Dozier was the one to run the football into the end zone, Conlan’s pick was a 38-yard return to the 6-yard line, setting up Dozier’s inside rush for the go-ahead score.

He was later named an All-American for the second consecutive year and was a finalist for the Butkus Award given to the nation’s top linebacker. He was the highest drafted Penn State player in the 1987 NFL Draft, going No. 8 overall to the Buffalo Bills and eventually became a three-time All-Pro selection.

Interceptions: Duffy Cobbs

In the opening moments of the second quarter of the Fiesta Bowl, Testaverde had plenty of time to maneuver out of the pocket and send the ball upfield. However, Duffy Cobbs, who led Penn State in interceptions that season, positioned himself in the right place at the right time, easily picking off the pass on a potential scoring drive.

Cobbs had four interceptions on the season, including his grab in the Fiesta Bowl and another crucial interception against Syracuse in the Homecoming game on the first play of the matchup.

He went on to sign with the New England Patriots as an undrafted free agent following the 1987 NFL Draft, appearing in three games.

Kicking: Massimo Manca

Despite putting in a down year in terms of production compared to his first season as the full-time starter, Massimo Manca still set the record for most points scored by a kicker at the time in 1986 with 206 in his career. That record has since slid to the No. 14 spot.

In the 1986 season, Manca converted all 37 of his extra point attempts, but went 14-23 on field goals to cap off a relatively turbulent collegiate career. Three of his field goal completions came in the victory over Alabama, padding the lead for a convincing win.

In the Fiesta Bowl, Manca went 2-for-2 on extra points with a missed field goal from 50 yards out. His second extra point pushed the lead to 14-10 to ward off a potential chance at overtime, as Miami was left with no choice but to go for the touchdown on the last play of the game.

Manca went on to sign with the Cincinnati Bengals as an undrafted free agent and bounced around the league for the next two years on practice squads before finishing his career with the Barcelona Dragons of the World League of American Football, otherwise known as NFL Europe.

title: 2027 TE Sean Currie commits to Penn State
author: Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has its second tight end commit in the Class of 2027.

Sean Currie announced his commitment to Penn State on Friday via the Rivals YouTube channel. He was formerly committed to Virginia for both football and lacrosse, but backed off his pledge in March.

Currie, a 6-foot-7, 230-pounder out of Lawrenceville, New Jersey, ranks as the No. 15 player in his home state and the No. 32 tight end in his class, per 247Sports composite.

The 3-star recruit chose the Nittany Lions over other elite Power Four programs such as Ohio State, Texas A&M, Vanderbilt and Georgia Tech.

In his junior year of high school at The Lawrenceville School, Currie had 34 receptions for 474 yards and eight touchdowns. He also played defensive back, recording three sacks and a pair of tackles for loss.

He has a date set for an official visit to Happy Valley on June 19. However, offensive coordinator Taylor Mouser, running backs coach Savon Huggins and quarterbacks coach Jake Waters all came out to visit him and his family for dinner on May 8.

He joins Cooper Terwilliger as the second tight end in the 2027 class.

title: Penn State LB Tony Rojas named to Lott IMPACT Trophy watch list

Penn State's top linebacker has been recognized for his abilities on the field as well as his character off the field.

Tony Rojas was named to the Lott IMPACT Trophy watch list on Friday, given annually to the nation's best defensive player of the year who displays traits of its namesake, Pro Football Hall of Famer Ronnie Lott.

Rojas, if selected as winner, would follow in the footsteps of former Nittany Lion Carl Nassib, who won the award in 2015.

title: Film review | 3-star RB Chukwuma Odoh brings speed, strength to Penn State
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
3-star Chukwuma Odoh became the second running back commit in a week, pledging to the Nittany Lions last Thursday and capping off a big week for the future of Penn State’s running back room.

Odoh, the No. 57-ranked back in his class, possesses both speed and toughness in his run game. Meanwhile, 4-star commit Aiden Gibson brings more downhill running and receiving.

While Odoh missed a pair of games during his junior season, he still made a big impact on the field with 929 yards and 13 touchdowns. That was a down year, however, when compared to his sophomore season during which he ran for 1,418 yards, including 10 100-yard games and four games that broke for over 150 yards.

Here’s a look at what the New Jersey native can bring to the Nittany Lions’ running back room.

Downfield speed

On the field and on the track, Odoh shows quick speed that’s capable of taking him to the end zone. Throughout his track career, he’s posted times of 10.86 seconds in the 100-meters and 22.34 seconds in the 200-meters, showcasing his raw speed.

During his junior year, Odoh rushed for nearly 10 yards per carry. Much of that success came from his speed, which allowed him to get off the line of scrimmage and down the field quickly.

As soon as Odoh has the ball in his hands, he bolts off the line and is quickly down the field.Here’s Odoh taking the hand off and immediately getting 15 yards downfield before he faces much defense: pic.twitter.com/Y9MOfOlr30— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Not only does Odoh have the speed to be successful as a running back, but he’s doing it at 6-foot, 220 pounds. Odoh is comparable to Nick Singleton’s size and quickness on the field.

While Odoh is bursting out of the backfield, he pushes past several defenders as he fights for the first down. It took six defenders to take him down. pic.twitter.com/rSREMHrL1i— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Despite being ranked 35 spots behind Gibson, Odoh’s speed could pair well with Gibson’s skillset to provide another backfield duo in the likes of Singleton and Kaytron Allen.

Big-gain playmaking

In addition to Odoh finding the end zone 13 times during his junior season, the young star had many more highlight-reel plays.

One of the biggest games of his career came against Immaculata in the NJSIAA Non-Public B quarterfinals, where he scored five touchdowns in his team’s 63-31 victory. A week later, he rushed for 301 yards and three touchdowns against Red Bank Catholic in the semifinals, showing he can perform on the biggest stages.

In the semifinals, Odoh utilized a juke move, stiff arm and spin move to move down the field before being pushed out of bounds pic.twitter.com/sIsp6bBwQ8— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Odoh’s pass-catching skills were infrequently used during his first three years at Paramus Catholic. But when they were used, he continued to do what he does best and made more big plays.

He recorded five catches for 62 yards and a touchdown during his junior season, an increase from his sophomore campaign when he had just 55 yards and no score.

Odoh takes the short pass for another 35 yards after the catch, showcasing his ability to make big plays pic.twitter.com/gJ2ZiPB1fq— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

While Odoh doesn't have a lot of experience pass-catching, he's displayed he can perform by air or ground and could be a successful option through the air for the Nittany Lions.

High IQ play

Not only does Odoh have the size, speed and determination to get into the end zone, but he also has the IQ to make that scoring possible.

When he’s not rushing full steam ahead, Odoh plays with a Le’Veon Bell-style rushing attack, pausing at the line of scrimmage before making his move down the field.

After evaluating his options downfield, Odoh takes the ball the entire length of the field sideways to earn the first down, running much more than the roughly 10 yards on the stat sheet pic.twitter.com/ABsJTOlDmT— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

Even with size and speed playing to his advantage, Odoh doesn’t necessarily charge headfirst at defenders. Instead, he’s shown quick thinking as he finds the best gaps to make a play downfield.

In a crowded endzone, Odoh found his way through a tight gap between two blockers, adding another score to his stat sheet pic.twitter.com/pwuSx7mSLB— Collegian Football (@psufootblog) May 21, 2026

While he’s only a 3-star back and ranked as the No. 57 player at his position in the nation, Odoh has showcased his quickness, decision-making and toughness, giving him the potential to be a difference maker for the Nittany Lions.

title: Report: Penn State baseball's Nate Voss, Jack Porter enter transfer portal

title: Penn State men's lacrosse earns 4 USILA All-Americans
author: Andrew Deal | Sports Editor
description:
After a strong season, Penn State has earned some individual accolades.

Defenseman Alex Ross was named a First-Team All-American. Short-stick defenseman Jon King and attacker Hunter Aquino were both named to the third team, with Reid Gills earning honorable mention for his faceoff controls.

Award season continues…Alex Ross - 𝗙𝗶𝗿𝘀𝘁 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺Jon King - 𝗧𝗵𝗶𝗿𝗱 𝗧𝗲𝗮𝗺Hunter Aquino - 𝗛𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻Reid Gills - 𝗛𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗿𝗮𝗯𝗹𝗲 𝗠𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 pic.twitter.com/F8DBcKrE4x— Penn State Men’s Lacrosse (@PennStateMLAX) May 21, 2026

Ross was the Nittany Lions' top defenseman, often locking down the opposing team's biggest threat on the offensive end. In addition to his on-ball defense, he totaled 35 ground balls, the most of his career.

King posed another threat on the defensive end, earning 38 ground balls. He turned defense into offense, scoring five goals and an assist across 16 games this year. Aquino, despite dealing with injuries all season long, scored 26 goals and nine assists, with a 39.4% shot percentage.

At the faceoff circle, Gills finished 10th in the country with a .612 winning percentage on faceoffs, going 123-78 on the season.

Gills and Aquino will return for their third season with the blue and white, while Ross and King will both leave the team after ending their senior campaigns.

title: Penn State men’s lacrosse earns commitment from graduate transfer Owen Tasse
author: Liana Tiamzon | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has added a new defenseman to its roster.

Owen Tasse committed to the Nittany Lions on Thursday, he announced on Instagram.

Tasse is a veteran player from St. Bonaventure that was brought in as one of the Atlantic 10’s top takeaway defenders.

The Ottawa, Ontario native appeared in 14 games for the Bonnies in 2026, causing 30 turnovers and scooping 59 ground balls. Those marks ranked third and seventh in the conference per game, respectively.

The graduate transfer also contributed offensively, notching three goals and an assist in his collegiate career.

Before St. Bonaventure, Tasse built an impressive résumé in Canada, helping his teams win multiple city championships and national box lacrosse titles, including the 2023 Minto Cup with the Burlington Blaze and the 2022 Founders Cup with the Nepean Knights. His father, Jason Tasse, also played professionally in the National Lacrosse League.

Tasse now heads to Happy Valley to add some new physicality and depth to Penn State’s defensive unit entering the 2027 season. He'll have a chance to compete for minutes, with longtime starter Alex Ross off to the PLL.

title: Indoor pickleball franchise set to open in State College
author: Taylor Mcgillvray | The Daily Collegian
description:
The Picklr, a rapidly expanding indoor pickleball franchise, announced plans to open its third Pennsylvania facility in State College this summer.

The 27,000-square-foot club will be located in the Benner Pike Shops at 503 Benner Pike, in the former Big Lots building across from the new Happy Valley Casino.

The facility will feature nine indoor courts, a pro shop and a private event space. The courts will use the same surfacing as those used by The Professional Pickleball Association.

Former Penn State football player Steve Smith, class of 1999, will own and operate the State College location. Smith said returning to the area and opening the facility is especially meaningful because of his connection to Penn State.

Members will have access to open play, leagues, tournaments and training programs through The Picklr Academy, which offers instruction for players of all skill levels. Memberships will also include access to Picklr locations nationwide.

The State College location is part of the company’s continued national expansion, with more than 500 clubs and 5,000 courts currently in development across North America.

Prospective players can purchase memberships or book individual courts once the facility opens.

title: Recapping the careers of Penn State women’s lacrosse’s 6 seniors
author: Noah Aberegg | The Daily Collegian
description:
After four years of repping the blue and white, several Penn Staters took the field for the final time two weeks ago, capping off their collegiate careers.

From being named to the Tewaaraton Award watch list to earning First Team All-Big Ten, here’s a look at the legacies the six Nittany Lions left on their team.

While defender Maggie Driver is a senior, she announced her return for a fifth year on May 13 and won’t be discussed in detail.

Lauren Tinson

After taking on a bigger role with the team last season, defender Lauren Tinson stepped up to the plate her senior year. Tinson was named a team captain and started all 19 games for the first time in her career.

The Downingtown, Pennsylvania, native was among the best defenders on the team, sitting third with 23 caused turnovers in Year 4. In eight games this year, she recorded multiple turnovers, proving to be a crucial part of the defensive stand.

With 27 career starts through four seasons, Tinson wrapped up her collegiate career with a total of 31 ground balls and 28 caused turnovers.

Erika Ho

Erika Ho was a go-to attacker throughout her time at Penn State, from scoring one goal her freshman year to a career-high 46 goals her junior year, earning All-Big Ten First Team and IWLCA West/Midwest All-Region First Team honors.

After being named captain for her senior year and selected to the Tewaaraton Award watch list, Ho sustained an injury that sidelined her for a pair of games and off the starting lineup for another six games. However, she still finished Year 4 with the fourth-most goals on the team, tallying 35 goals and 14 assists.

Not only did the Frankfort, Illinois, native succeed on offense, but she was also dominant at the draw. During her junior year, she picked up 112 draw controls, adding another 17 her senior year.

Payton Wainman

While attacker Payton Wainman found success early in her career with 12 starts her sophomore year and seven as a junior, she took her performance to another level in her final campaign.

The Delray Beach, Florida, native finished Year 4 with 43 goals, good for third-best on the team. In addition to her high-scoring output, Wainman proved to be an efficient shooter, recording the second-best shot-on-goal percentage for players who scored 10-plus goals.

Wainman recorded six hat tricks during her final season in Happy Valley, including a career-high five goals against Drexel.

Emma Kelly

After sitting behind Ho on the draw control depth chart last season, attacker Emma Kelly took over at midfield this year, recording 120 draws.

While she was never the leading goal scorer on the team, Kelly still finished her collegiate career with 67 goals. The Bethpage, New York, native also found success causing turnovers from the offensive end, ending this season with the fifth-most caused turnovers on the team and her career with 23 turnovers after four years.

Maddie Greco

Midfielder Maddie Greco didn’t see the field much prior to her senior season, playing in just 22 games through three years. However, she started in all 19 games in Year 4 and made a big impact on the team.

The 5-foot, speedy midfielder tallied 10 goals and five assists, along with 11 ground balls, five caused turnovers and 11 draw controls her senior year — all career highs. Of her 10 goals, three came against Bucknell, where she recorded her only career hat trick.

Emily Bette

Goalkeeper Emily Bette has only played in three games during her four years at Penn State, recording just 16 minutes and 49 seconds of playing time.

Despite her limited time on the field, Bette wrapped up her career with a .500 save percentage, including three saves her senior year.

title: Penn State men's volleyball names Colin McMillan as next head coach
author: Will Horstman | The Daily Collegian
description:
Penn State has its next head coach following Mark Pavlik's retirement.

The Nittany Lions promoted associate head coach Colin McMillan to the head coaching role, the team announced Wednesday.

Colin’s 20th year in Happy Valley will be as head coach!He is the fourth head coach in the 50-year history of the program. #WeAre pic.twitter.com/SWGMkS4dtz— Penn State Men's Volleyball (@PennStateMVBALL) May 20, 2026

"I am honored and humbled to be given the opportunity to be the next head coach of Penn State men's volleyball,” McMillan said in a news release. “To continue the legacy established by coach (Tom) Tait, coach (Tom) Peterson and coach Pavlik is a huge responsibility that I look forward to taking on. I would like to thank Dr. (Pat) Kraft and the administration for believing in our staff to lead this program and build upon the great foundation laid by all those that have come before us."

McMillan has been on Penn State's staff for the past 19 seasons, and the last 10 years have been as associate head coach. With McMillan on staff, the Nittany Lions won a national title in 2008, 14 EIVA Tournament championships and nine runs to the Final Four.

The middle blocker position has been McMillan's expertise on staff. He's helped coach Olympian Max Holt, who won a bronze medal with Team USA in the 2024 Olympics, and most recently Owen Rose, who is a three-time All-American.

“We are thrilled to name Colin as the next head coach of the men’s volleyball program at Penn State,” Kraft said in a news release. “He has played an integral role in the team’s success at the conference and national level in his nearly two decades working with coach Pavlik. His dedication to the program and his commitment to developing student-athletes at the collegiate level made him an easy choice to ensure Penn State continues to compete as a championship-caliber team.”

McMillan was a middle blocker during his playing days at Ohio State in the early 2000s, where he was a four-time All-MIVA selection and made the 2000 NC Championship All-Tournament team. He then started his coaching career as a graduate assistant for the Buckeyes in 2003.

Now, McMillan will become the fourth head coach in the history of Penn State's program.

"I am so grateful and so fortunate to have worked alongside Colin for the last 19 years,” Pavlik said in a news release. “He has been an outstanding role model for our guys as a coach, father and husband. His integrity, compassion and leadership have served our program so well. He has helped to mold many minds and lives - mine included. Now, as he becomes the next head coach of Penn State men's volleyball, I am thrilled to know our program will continue to improve. He knows and understands better than anyone the culture of this program, our student-athletes and the traditions which make this team special to all who came before. The future of Penn State men's volleyball is in great hands!"

title: Dueling column | Who should Penn State play in the 2026 White Out?
author: Noah Aberegg & Andrew Mercer | The Daily Collegian
description:
The 2026 season boasts a lot of changes for Penn State — a new head coach, a new quarterback and an almost completed set of stadium renovations.

But one thing this season will look the same as always. The Nittany Lions have held the White Out in some capacity for the past 22 years, drawing in some of the biggest crowds of all time to Happy Valley for some of the most anticipated matchups of the year.

Here are two games that Penn State could circle on the calendar for its annual White Out.

USC (Noah Aberegg)

Of the five home Big Ten games Penn State has scheduled this season, USC had the best record last year, and it’s also the only home opponent who finished the season ranked in the AP poll.

In a battle of two teams from opposite ends of the country, there’s been only nine matchups between the two sides, but the two most recent games have been instant classics — the Trojans’ comeback in the 2017 Rose Bowl and the Nittany Lions overtime win in 2024.

Considering that recent history, having USC as the White Out opponent is an easy choice.

While the game won’t be a top-10 matchup like last year’s record-breaking White Out between Oregon and Penn State, it could feature two teams who were ranked in ESPN’s latest top 25, which was released in mid-April.

Not only would it be a good matchup on the field, but having the White Out against USC could also highlight a new connection between the two teams in defensive coordinator D’Anton Lynn.

After spending two years with the Trojans, Lynn was signed as Penn State’s next defensive coordinator in December. Not only did he experience the Nittany Lions’ nail-biting victory in 2024, but he played for Penn State from 2008-11, playing against the Trojans in 2009.

A White Out game against his previous team would be a special homecoming for Lynn by coaching his alma mater against the program he previously coached for.

Minnesota (Andrew Mercer)

When it comes to Penn State’s relationship with Minnesota, there’s an often overlooked historical piece involved that would make this season’s White Out all the more meaningful — the Governor’s Victory Bell trophy.

Therefore, contrary to popular opinion, I believe that Penn State should host Minnesota for the annual White Out game, especially considering the close finish between the two in 2024.

The pair first met in 1993, which was the Nittany Lions’ first-ever Big Ten conference game in program history. With Campbell coming to Happy Valley as the new head coach, playing the same opponent that Penn State played in its Big Ten debut as his first White Out would be poetic.

Additionally, Minnesota would be an easier team for Campbell’s White Out debut compared to USC. The Golden Gophers went 5-4 in the Big Ten last season, and Minnesota’s over/under on FanDuel is set to 6.5 wins this regular season, with -158 odds of the Golden Gophers winning less than that figure.

There’s not only the feat of winning Penn State’s signature home game on the line, but the fact that it’s Campbell’s first means he’ll likely wish to make the best impression on the fanbase.

The best way of doing so would be emerging victorious, something Penn State has done in a White Out against Minnesota previously. The Nittany Lions hosted the Golden Gophers in the annual spectacle in 2022, emerging with a 45-17 victory.

On the spectator side of things, there’s the possibility that broadcast rights to the USC game would be picked up by Fox’s Big Noon Kickoff. Nearly all prior White Out games were played at night, with the special exception of when Penn State faced SMU in the College Football Playoffs at noon.

With that said, should Fox claim the USC game and the time be set for noon, that would put Minnesota in prime position to be Penn State’s White Out opponent.

The only wrinkle with Minnesota as the White Out opponent is the date of the matchup. Because it's currently scheduled for Nov. 14, that means the Golden Gophers would have to agree to play at night, something they might choose not to do to avoid the White Out crowd.

Regardless, playing Minnesota in the White Out would be an easy way for Campbell to have a successful first experience coaching in that atmosphere.

title: Results are in for 2026 PA primary elections | Here's who will be on the Centre County ballot in November
author: Anisa Daniel-Oniko | The Daily Collegian
description:
Centre County voters cast their ballots in the 2026 Pennsylvania primary elections, with polls closing at 8:00 p.m. on Tuesday.

A total of 20,315 ballots were cast for the 2026 Gubernatorial Primary, representing 24.37% of the county’s 83,360 registered voters, according to centrecountypa.gov.

Below are unofficial results of the 2026 Pennsylvania primary election as of 11:16 p.m. on Tuesday.

Democratic Governor

Incumbent Josh Shapiro ran uncontested for the Democratic ticket, receiving 11,443 total votes with 135 write-ins. Shapiro, the 48th governor of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, was first sworn into the Office of the Governor in 2023.

Republican Governor

Stacy Garrity, the Republican candidate for the gubernatorial nomination, ran uncontested and received 7,205 total votes, with 752 write-ins.

Garrity, a retired U.S. Army Reserve Colonel, served as the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania’s 78th treasurer.

Democratic Lieutenant Governor

Incumbent Austin Davis, who also ran uncontested for the Democratic nomination, received 11,314 total votes with 64 write-ins. Davis, who is the youngest Lieutenant Governor in the county, is also the first Black Lieutenant Governor.

The lieutenant governor serves as chair of the state Board of Pardons alongside the attorney general and three governor-appointed additional members, reviewing criminal cases to decide if a pardon or commutation should be suggested to the governor for final approval or denial, according to the state’s constitution.

Additionally, Pennsylvania’s Lieutenant Governor serves as President of the State Senate.

Republican Lieutenant Governor

Jason Richey ran against John Ventre for the Republican nomination and received 5,190 votes in total, pulling ahead of Ventre by 2,723 votes. Richey, who has worked as a litigator for over thirty years, was endorsed by Republican gubernatorial candidate Garrity.

Ventre, who did not win the election, received 2,467 votes. He is a member of the Committee of Seventy, which is a nonpartisan civil leadership initiative focused on advancing representative government through civilian engagement and advocacy, according to the organization’s website.

Democratic Representative in Congress

Ray Bilger ran uncontested for the Democratic nomination and received 11,412 total votes with 57 write-ins. Bilger, who has led teams for the Air Force, National Security Agency and State Department, was the NSA Technical Director for Cyber Analysis from 2015 to 2017.

Republican Representative in Congress

Incumbent Glenn “GT” Thompson ran uncontested for the Republican nomination and received 7,759 total votes with 258 write-ins. Thompson, who is a Penn State alum, currently represents the Fifteenth District in the House of Representatives.

Democratic Representative in the General Assembly 77th Legislative District

Incumbent Scott Conklin ran uncontested for the Democratic nomination and received 4,655 total votes with 34 write-ins. Conklin, who has been a member of the House of Representatives since 2006, was a primary sponsor of a bill that aimed to regulate the sale of land belonging to former state correctional institutions.

Republican Representative in the General Assembly 77th Legislative District

Michelle Schellberg also ran uncontested for the Republic nomination, receiving 2,501 total votes with 60 write-ins. Schellberg currently serves as chairwoman for the Center County Republican committee.

Democratic Representative in the General Assembly 82nd Legislative District

Incumbent Paul Takac ran uncontested and received 4,497 total votes with 13 write-ins for the Democratic nomination. Takac has represented the 82nd legislation district—which includes Penn State’s University Park campus—since 2022. On Oct. 1, 2025, he spoke at a Harrisburg press conference given by Conklin and Rep. Dallas Kephart, cosponsors of the aforementioned bill aiming to regulate the sale of former SCI land.

Republican Representative in the General Assembly 82nd Legislative District

Kirsten McTernan ran uncontested for the Republican nomination and received 2,644 total votes with 89 write-ins. She ran for the State College Area School Board in 2025.

Democratic Representative in the General Assembly 171st Legislative District

John Zangari, who ran unopposed for the Democratic nomination, received 2,220 total votes with 18 write-ins. In the past, Zangari served as a Lehigh County corrections officer, and he currently works as the Administrative and Facilities Assistant in Penn State’s Department of Sociology and Criminology at University Park.

Republican Representative in the General Assembly 171st Legislative District

Incumbent Kerry Benninghoff ran uncontested for the Republican nomination and received 2,524 total votes with 38 write-ins. Benninghoff, who currently represents the 171st Legislative District, also serves as Republican chairman of the House Transportation Committee. He is also a member of the Gaming Oversight and Rules committees.

The full list of Centre County unofficial General Primary Elections results is available at centrecountypa.gov. The next step for these candidates is the November 3 general elections.
